subsystem: QPDFWriter::enqueueObject — the object-graph enqueuing routine of qpdf's PDF writer. It walks a QPDFObjectHandle (a PDF object: indirect reference, array, dictionary, stream, or scalar), assigns it a new output object number, and pushes it onto the write queue, recursing into container members so the whole reachable object graph is scheduled for writing.
provenance: Identified from the fully-qualified method name QPDFWriter::enqueueObject and the QPDF/QPDFObjectHandle/QPDFObjGen types (qpdf library, Jay Berkenbilt). The QTC::TC("qpdf", ...) test-coverage instrumentation macro, the copyForeignObject reference in the logic_error message, and members like obj_renumber, object_queue, next_objid, object_to_object_stream, object_stream_to_objects, linearized, and direct_stream_lengths are all qpdf's QPDFWriter internals. The header banner names the file corpus/train/train_0157_cwe399.cpp and the sample tag primevul_train_2209_vuln indicates a resource-management (CWE-399) sample from PrimeVul.
trust_boundary: The QPDFObjectHandle graph originates from a parsed input PDF, which is fully attacker-controlled. An attacker crafts the file's object structure: nesting depth of direct arrays/dictionaries, the number and cross-references of indirect objects, and object-stream membership. Everything reached through object.getArrayItem, object.getKey, and pdf.getObjectByID is untrusted structure derived from the file. The renumbering/queue bookkeeping (obj_renumber, object_queue, next_objid) is trusted internal state, but its termination and bounds depend entirely on the untrusted shape of the object graph.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The method takes a single QPDFObjectHandle by value. A QPDFObjectHandle is qpdf's polymorphic handle to any PDF object node (indirect reference, array, dictionary, stream, or scalar). enqueueObject is the traversal driver of the writer: for every object it decides how the object should be numbered and queued for output, and it recurses into container objects so the transitive closure of the object graph gets scheduled. Because it calls itself (lines 25, 58, 70), the call depth is a direct function of the input file's structure.

The first branch handles indirect objects (objects addressed by an object/generation number and stored once in the file). It enforces a safety invariant: the object must belong to the QPDF being written (this->pdf). If getOwningQPDF() points at a different QPDF, the code emits a test-coverage marker and throws std::logic_error telling the caller to use QPDF::copyForeignObject. This is a defensive guard against mixing objects across documents, not attacker-triggerable from a single file's contents.

og is the object's (object number, generation) identity. obj_renumber is the map from original QPDFObjGen to newly assigned output object number. The guard obj_renumber.count(og) == 0 means "only process this indirect object if it has not already been renumbered." This is the once-guard that makes the traversal terminate over the indirect-object graph: an indirect object that is reachable through many paths (or through a reference cycle among indirect objects) is renumbered and queued exactly once, so indirect-to-indirect cycles cannot loop forever here.

If og is stored inside a compressed object stream, the object is not queued directly; instead the containing object stream (looked up by stream_id, always generation 0) is fetched via pdf.getObjectByID and enqueueObject is called on it recursively. This is the third recursion site. Note that in this branch obj_renumber[og] is NOT set for the original og — only the stream object it recurses into will later be renumbered — so the once-guard at line 17 does not record og as seen when it lives in an object stream.

For an indirect object not in an object stream, the object is appended to object_queue (the ordered list the writer will later emit) and assigned the next available output object id via obj_renumber[og] = next_objid++. Setting obj_renumber[og] here is what satisfies the line-17 guard on any subsequent visit, ensuring each such object is queued once. next_objid is a running integer counter of output object numbers.

If this object is itself an object stream (generation 0 and present as a key in object_stream_to_objects, which maps a stream's object number to the set of objects it contains), then unless the file is being linearized, assignCompressedObjectNumbers(og) is called to allocate output numbers for all the objects packed into that stream. For linearized output the numbering of uncompressed objects is deferred and handled elsewhere, so this step is skipped.

Otherwise, if stream /Length values are written as indirect objects (direct_stream_lengths is false) and this object is a stream, an extra output object id is reserved (++next_objid) to hold that stream's length as a separate indirect object. This is bookkeeping that keeps next_objid consistent with the number of objects that will actually be emitted.

For a DIRECT array (a container held inline, not by reference), the code reads the item count n = getArrayNItems() and iterates, recursively enqueuing each element via enqueueObject(object.getArrayItem(i)) when not linearizing. This is the primary recursion site for container descent: each element may itself be a direct array or dictionary, so the recursion depth follows the nesting depth of direct containers in the input file. There is no depth counter, no visited-set for direct nodes, and no cap on n other than the loop bound.

For a DIRECT dictionary, the code collects the key set and recurses into each value via enqueueObject(object.getKey(*iter)) when not linearizing. Same as the array case, this descends the graph and its recursion depth equals the nesting depth of direct dictionaries/arrays, with no depth bound. Note getKeys builds a std::set<std::string> copy on the stack at every level, so each recursion frame also carries a heap-backed container, increasing per-frame memory pressure during deep recursion.

Scalars (integers, names, strings, booleans, null) fall through to the empty else and are ignored: they carry no children and need no numbering here. This is the recursion base case — recursion only unwinds when every path bottoms out at a scalar or at an already-renumbered indirect object.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion. For direct arrays the function recurses with no depth limit. An attacker builds a PDF whose object graph nests direct arrays thousands of levels deep (e.g. [[[[ ... ]]]]). Because a direct array is descended immediately (line 58), enqueueObject re-enters once per nesting level; the C++ call stack grows one frame per level until it overflows and the process crashes (SIGSEGV). This is a remotely triggerable denial of service against any service that opens untrusted PDFs with qpdf's writer. The obj_renumber once-guard at line 17 only fires for INDIRECT objects, so it does not bound the depth of direct-container nesting at all. The safe contract is an explicit recursion-depth limit or an iterative worklist traversal that rejects (or safely caps) pathologically deep structures before descending.

Same uncontrolled-recursion class via direct dictionaries, and worse per-frame cost. Each level allocates a std::set<std::string> copy of the key names (line 64) that lives for the duration of that frame, so a deeply nested chain of direct dictionaries consumes both stack frames and heap-backed containers as it descends. A crafted file mixing deep direct dictionaries and arrays reaches stack overflow at least as fast as pure arrays. The invariant to uphold is identical: bound traversal depth independent of the file-controlled nesting.

Object-stream recursion without a per-og seen-mark. When og lives in an object stream, the code recurses into the stream object (line 25) but never sets obj_renumber[og] for og itself; the once-guard is only satisfied for the stream object it descends into. If the same compressed og is reached again through another path, the guard at line 17 is still false and the stream lookup+recurse runs again. The stream object itself is only queued once (its obj_renumber gets set at line 30), so this is redundant work rather than a hard infinite loop in the normal case, but it depends on object_to_object_stream / getObjectByID returning consistent, terminating data. If a malformed stream_id maps an object stream to (transitively) contain itself, or getObjectByID resolves back into an object-stream membership that points at another stream indefinitely, this recursion could fail to terminate. The safe contract is to mark og as visited before recursing so each compressed object triggers the stream enqueue at most once.

Output-object-id counter overflow. next_objid is a plain signed int incremented here and again at line 46 (++next_objid) for reserved stream-length ids. A file engineered to enqueue more than INT_MAX objects (or, combined with reserved-length ids, roughly half that) overflows next_objid to a negative value, producing invalid/duplicate output object numbers and undefined behavior on signed overflow. This requires an enormous but not impossible object count and is a lower-severity integrity/DoS concern rather than memory corruption. The invariant is that next_objid must be a wide unsigned type and/or be bounded against a sane maximum object count.

summary: enqueueObject recursively descends the attacker-controlled PDF object graph with no recursion-depth limit: direct arrays (lines 51-60) and direct dictionaries (lines 62-72) are followed one call frame per nesting level, so a deeply nested crafted PDF exhausts the stack and crashes the process — a remotely triggerable denial of service (uncontrolled recursion, CWE-674, the CWE-399 resource-management class this sample is tagged with). The obj_renumber once-guard only terminates cycles among indirect objects and does nothing to bound direct-container depth. Secondary concerns are the object-stream recursion that does not mark the compressed object as seen (line 25) and signed overflow of next_objid.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Recurses at lines 25, 58, 70 with no depth bound; central uncontrolled-recursion surface.
Type-dispatch predicate; selects the indirect-object handling path, no direct security contract.
Defensive same-document check that throws logic_error; a safety guard, not attacker-triggerable via file contents.
Programmer-error path for cross-document objects; not reachable from a single untrusted file.
Reads object identity; input to the once-guard, carries no standalone hazard.
Terminates indirect-object cycles only; does NOT bound direct-container recursion depth — the reason the DoS exists.
Recurses into the object stream without marking og seen; redundant/potentially non-terminating on malformed stream membership.
Appends to output queue; growth is bounded by distinct renumbered objects, no per-call overflow.
Signed int output-id counter can overflow with an extreme object count; integrity/DoS.
Delegates compressed-object numbering to another routine (not shown); no local memory contract in this file.
Reserves one id; folds into the same next_objid overflow concern already captured.
Primary unbounded-recursion descent into direct arrays; stack exhaustion.
Unbounded-recursion descent into direct dictionaries plus per-frame key-set allocation.
Base case for scalars; correctly does nothing, no contract to violate.

findings.title: Uncontrolled recursion over the untrusted PDF object graph causes stack-exhaustion DoS
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled recursion / stack exhaustion (denial of service)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0157_cwe399.cpp (this function); depth is unbounded here regardless of the rest of the implementation
findings.invariant: Traversal depth must be bounded independently of file-controlled nesting — e.g. an explicit recursion-depth counter with a hard cap, or an iterative worklist/stack-based traversal — so a maliciously deep object graph is rejected or capped rather than driving native call-stack growth.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker crafts a PDF whose direct arrays/dictionaries nest thousands of levels deep. enqueueObject recurses once per level (lines 58 and 70) with no depth limit; the once-guard at line 17 only applies to indirect objects and does not restrain direct-container depth. The native call stack overflows and the process receives SIGSEGV, crashing any service that writes/optimizes untrusted PDFs. This is remotely triggerable with a single small file (nesting is far cheaper to encode than the stack it consumes).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no ambient recursion-depth guard around enqueueObject in QPDFWriter, and that direct arrays/dictionaries can be nested arbitrarily by the parser (QPDFObjectHandle) without a prior depth cap. Verify whether the caller pre-limits nesting; if not, the DoS is reachable.

findings.title: Object-stream recursion does not mark the compressed object as visited
findings.bug_class: Missing once-guard on recursion (redundant / potentially non-terminating processing)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0157_cwe399.cpp (this branch), with termination also depending on object_to_object_stream / getObjectByID in the implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Before recursing into the containing object stream, og itself should be recorded as seen (or the recursion must be provably bounded by consistent object_to_object_stream membership), so each compressed object triggers the stream enqueue at most once and no membership relation can make the descent re-enter indefinitely.
findings.failure_mode: For a compressed object, obj_renumber[og] is never set in this branch, so re-reaching the same og through another path re-runs the lookup and recursion. In the normal case the stream is queued only once (its obj_renumber is set at line 30) so this is wasted work, but if a malformed file makes object_to_object_stream / getObjectByID resolve an object stream that (transitively) maps back into object-stream membership, the recursion may fail to terminate, compounding the stack-exhaustion DoS.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether an object stream can itself be reported as living inside an object stream (object_to_object_stream keyed by a stream's own og), and whether repeated enqueueObject calls for the same compressed og are possible along distinct graph paths.

findings.title: Signed output-object-id counter (next_objid) can overflow
findings.bug_class: Signed integer overflow of object-number counter
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0157_cwe399.cpp (increments here and at line 46); declaration of next_objid in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: next_objid must be a sufficiently wide unsigned counter and/or bounded against a sane maximum object count, so assigned output object numbers never wrap to negative or collide.
findings.failure_mode: next_objid is a signed int incremented at line 30 and again at line 46. A file engineered to enqueue on the order of INT_MAX objects overflows it, which is undefined behavior for signed types and yields invalid/duplicate output object numbers, corrupting the written file's cross-reference or crashing. High object count required, so severity is low, but it is attacker-influenced.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check the declared type/width of next_objid and whether any upstream limit caps the total number of objects a single input file may contribute before enqueueObject runs.

audit_checklist: Is there ANY recursion-depth limit around QPDFWriter::enqueueObject? If not, confirm a deeply nested direct array/dictionary PDF crashes the writer with a stack overflow.
Verify the obj_renumber once-guard (line 17) is only reached for indirect objects and provides no protection against direct-container nesting depth.

Determine whether a compressed object's og is ever marked visited; trace whether the same compressed og can re-enter the line-25 stream recursion via multiple paths or a self-referential object-stream membership.
Confirm getArrayNItems()/getKeys() cannot themselves be attacker-inflated to combine breadth (huge n) with depth into a memory/CPU DoS.
Check the declared type of next_objid and whether the total object count from one file is bounded before overflow of the id counter is possible (lines 30, 46).
Confirm the parser (QPDFObjectHandle) permits arbitrarily deep direct nesting without its own depth cap, since that is the source of the untrusted depth reaching enqueueObject.
